---
title: "Spring Caching: A Curated Reading & Viewing List Beyond Plöd's 2016 Talk"
category: "Spring & Spring Boot"
description: "Structured curriculum updating Michael Plöd's 2016 Spring Caching talk for a 2026 Kotlin/Spring Boot 3.x stack (Caffeine, Lettuce/Redis, Hibernate 6, Aurora) — conference talks, engineering blog posts, and primary sources organized by topic."
---

# Spring Caching: A Curated Reading & Viewing List Beyond Plöd's 2016 Talk

You've just watched Michael Plöd's *Caching with Spring: Advanced Topics and Best Practices* (Spring I/O 2016, [`youtube.com/watch?v=SpQzWtqulhM`](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SpQzWtqulhM)). The talk is now nine years old, predates Caffeine's mainstream adoption, treats Hazelcast/EhCache as the dominant data grid choices, and operates almost exclusively against Spring Framework 4 with the JCache spec as the unifying abstraction. Plöd's central thesis — that you should program against `org.springframework.cache.Cache` rather than tie yourself to a provider — has aged extremely well, but almost everything below the abstraction (cache topologies, consistency tactics, eviction algorithms, observability tooling, and the Hibernate/Spring Cache split) has changed substantially. This document is the structured curriculum for catching up.

The user is a mid-level Kotlin/Spring Boot 3.x backend engineer running Aurora MySQL/PostgreSQL, Redis (Lettuce), Hibernate 6, Kafka, and EKS/ECS, who has already studied Spring Cache internals. Material has been chosen accordingly — no "what is `@Cacheable`" tutorials, no Hello-World guides.

---

## A. Conference Talks (newest first)

### Talks updating Plöd's thesis directly

**Michael Plöd — "Caching with Spring: Advanced Topics and Best Practices" — Spring Meetup Munich 2017** ([youtube.com/watch?v=FW-ZQ7KCeu0](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FW-ZQ7KCeu0)). A re-record of the same talk you watched, but with an expanded Q&A and slightly cleaner audio. Worth skipping unless you want the live-meetup energy. The original SpringOne 2GX 2014 deck is on SlideShare ([`slideshare.net/mploed/spring-one-2-gx-2014-caching-with-spring-advanced-topics-and-best-practices`](https://www.slideshare.net/mploed/spring-one-2-gx-2014-caching-with-spring-advanced-topics-and-best-practices)) and is the canonical reference for his framing of topologies (heap / off-heap / clustered / replicating / invalidation-only) and the JCache discussion. As far as I can tell from a thorough search of his speaker history, Plöd has not given a follow-on caching talk; from 2018 onward his public speaking pivoted to Domain-Driven Design. Treat the 2014/2016/2017 talks as a single canonical artifact.

**Joris Kuipers — "It's all in the mix: producing production-ready apps with Spring Boot" — Spring I/O 2024** (search the *Spring I/O* YouTube channel; the talk is in the official 2024 playlist, demos at [`github.com/jkuipers`](https://github.com/jkuipers) under the matching repo). The closing-day talk you correctly flagged: Kuipers spends a meaningful chunk on caching alongside logging and error handling, with concrete advice on `@Cacheable` defaults, key design, transaction-cache interaction (`transactionAware()`), and not blindly trusting auto-config. Pairs well with Plöd because it explicitly covers the *production hardening* topics Plöd intentionally bracketed off ("I will NOT talk about latency / synchronization / what's the best caching product"); Kuipers is willing to opine.

**Vlad Mihalcea — "High-Performance Hibernate"** ([youtube.com/watch?v=BTdTEe9QL5k](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BTdTEe9QL5k); reprised at JPoint, Devoxx, and JavaZone). Hibernate-side complement: covers the second-level cache concurrency strategies (`READ_ONLY`, `NONSTRICT_READ_WRITE`, `READ_WRITE`, `TRANSACTIONAL`), why query cache is dangerous if you don't understand the N+1 it creates against the entity cache, and when 2LC is genuinely inferior to an explicit `@Cacheable` layer over a DTO projection. If you're using Hibernate 6 and Aurora, this is mandatory viewing because it answers the "Spring Cache vs Hibernate 2LC" question Plöd punted on. Mihalcea's blog posts at [`vladmihalcea.com/tag/second-level-cache/`](https://vladmihalcea.com/tag/second-level-cache/) and the dedicated chapter in *High-Performance Java Persistence* are the long-form versions.

**Mark Paluch — "Event-Driven Java Applications with Redis 5.0 Streams" — SpringOne** (InfoQ: [`infoq.com/presentations/event-driven-redis-spring/`](https://www.infoq.com/presentations/event-driven-redis-spring/)). Not strictly a caching talk, but Paluch is the Spring Data / Lettuce lead and the talk covers how to integrate Redis non-cache primitives (Streams, Pub/Sub) into a Spring application — directly relevant to building Pub/Sub cache-invalidation channels on top of `RedisMessageListenerContainer`. Pair with Spring Data Redis observability documentation at [`docs.spring.io/spring-data/redis/reference/observability.html`](https://docs.spring.io/spring-data/redis/reference/observability.html), which shows the Lettuce Micrometer tracing integration.

### Cache theory and modern eviction

**Ben Manes — Caffeine design talks** (the canonical written deck is [`highscalability.com/design-of-a-modern-cache/`](https://highscalability.com/design-of-a-modern-cache/), with the Caffeine wiki [`github.com/ben-manes/caffeine/wiki/Design`](https://github.com/ben-manes/caffeine/wiki/Design) and [`/wiki/Efficiency`](https://github.com/ben-manes/caffeine/wiki/Efficiency) as deeper companions). I could not find a recorded conference video of Manes presenting the W-TinyLFU material directly; he is more frequently a conference attendee than speaker. Treat his written work as the equivalent. The High Scalability post explains why Caffeine displaces both Guava and EhCache as the local-cache default in Spring Boot starters: Window TinyLFU achieves near-Bélády-optimal hit rates with 4-bit count-min-sketch counters at ~2 bytes overhead per entry, batched ring-buffer reads to eliminate per-op locking, and a hill-climbing window-size adapter. This is the single most important update to Plöd's "which cache do I pick" question — for a single-JVM tier, Caffeine is the answer.

**Salvatore Sanfilippo (antirez) — Redis design and Streams talks at RedisConf**. Antirez retired from Redis maintainership in 2020 and most of his recorded talks remain on Redis Inc.'s YouTube. The most relevant for your purposes is *The Design of Redis Streams* (RedisConf 2018), referenced in the LINE engineering write-up of RedisConf18 ([`engineering.linecorp.com/en/blog/presenting-on-redisconf18`](https://engineering.linecorp.com/en/blog/presenting-on-redisconf18)). His original Redlock proposal at [`redis.io/docs/latest/develop/clients/patterns/distributed-locks/`](https://redis.io/docs/latest/develop/clients/patterns/distributed-locks/) and the rebuttal exchange with Martin Kleppmann ([`martin.kleppmann.com/2016/02/08/how-to-do-distributed-locking.html`](https://martin.kleppmann.com/2016/02/08/how-to-do-distributed-locking.html)) form the definitive primary-source reading on whether you should use Redis to coordinate cache rebuilds. Kleppmann's verdict is essentially "use Redlock for efficiency optimization, never for correctness; require fencing tokens if correctness matters." This is critical context the moment you start considering "let me hold a Redis lock so only one pod rebuilds the cache."

### Production case studies (LINE, RedisConf)

**Hokuto Kagaya (LINE) — "Redis at LINE, 25 billion messages per day" — RedisConf 2018**. The slide deck is at [`slideshare.net/linecorp/redis-at-line-99471322`](https://www.slideshare.net/linecorp/redis-at-line-99471322); the speaker recap is at [`engineering.linecorp.com/en/blog/presenting-on-redisconf18`](https://engineering.linecorp.com/en/blog/presenting-on-redisconf18). The companion technical post on probabilistic early recomputation (PER) and Lua scripting for atomic cache rebuilds is at [`engineering.linecorp.com/en/blog/redis-lua-scripting-atomic-processing-cache/`](https://engineering.linecorp.com/en/blog/redis-lua-scripting-atomic-processing-cache/). LINE reported a 3× response-time improvement on cache-expiry events after adopting PER. This is a concrete data point for the *Wikipedia cache-stampede page*'s claim that XFetch is optimal. 🇰🇷-adjacent (LINE Engineering team).

**Lev Neiman & Jason Fan (DoorDash) — "How DoorDash Standardized and Improved Microservices Caching"** — written form, but the InfoQ summary at [`infoq.com/news/2023/10/doordash-multilayered-cache/`](https://www.infoq.com/news/2023/10/doordash-multilayered-cache/) is essentially a transcribed talk. Three-tier design: request-local `HashMap` → in-JVM Caffeine → Redis (Lettuce). The killer feature is per-layer *runtime control* (toggle on/off, change TTL, enable shadow mode comparing cached vs source-of-truth). This is the reference architecture you should mentally hold when a teammate says "let's add Redis"; it explicitly answers the cache-staleness, Redis-dependency, and observability gaps Plöd's 2014–2016 talk doesn't address. Pair with DoorDash's separate ML feature-store post ([`doordash.engineering/2022/05/03/how-we-applied-client-side-caching/`](https://doordash.engineering/2022/05/03/how-we-applied-client-side-caching/)) which reports a 70%+ cache hit rate from adding client-side caching in front of Redis.

### Suggested speakers from your list — what I could and couldn't find

I confirmed: Plöd's 2016 Spring I/O talk is his only recorded major caching session; Kuipers's 2024 Spring I/O talk is correctly identified; Mihalcea's *High-Performance Hibernate* covers 2LC explicitly; Paluch has SpringOne sessions on Redis Streams but not a dedicated caching talk; Manes's work is published more as papers and wiki essays than recorded conference talks. I did **not** find recorded talks specifically on Spring caching by Marc Gravell, Stéphane Nicoll, Juergen Hoeller, Oliver Drotbohm, Josh Long, Dan Vega, or Sébastien Deleuze — their caching content lives in blog posts and reference docs, not standalone conference sessions. Thorben Janssen's Hibernate caching content lives at [`thorben-janssen.com`](https://thorben-janssen.com) and his YouTube channel as short-form videos rather than conference keynotes.

---

## B. Engineering Blog Posts by Topic

### B1. Cache stampede / thundering herd / hot-key expiry

🇰🇷 **Toss — "캐시 문제 해결 가이드 - DB 과부하 방지 실전 팁"** ([toss.tech/article/cache-traffic-tip](https://toss.tech/article/cache-traffic-tip)). The single most useful Korean-language piece on this topic. Walks through (1) coordinated expiry storms (e.g. midnight TTL alignment) and the *jitter* mitigation, (2) hot-key cache miss with Redlock-via-Redisson distributed lock for single-rebuild guarantee, (3) cache penetration via negative-result caching. Concrete code, concrete reasoning. Use this as your "applied stampede" reference.

🇰🇷 **LINE Engineering — "Redis Lua script for atomic operations and cache stampede"** ([engineering.linecorp.com/en/blog/redis-lua-scripting-atomic-processing-cache/](https://engineering.linecorp.com/en/blog/redis-lua-scripting-atomic-processing-cache/)). Implements *probabilistic early recomputation* (XFetch / Vattani–Chierichetti–Lowenstein) on Redis using Lua. The atomic-`DEL`/`RPUSH`/`EXPIRE` race story is a great cautionary tale; their fix uses `MGET` + an additional "calculation cost" entry alongside TTL to drive the early-recompute decision. They report 3× improvement at full cache expiry.

**Wikipedia — Cache stampede** ([en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cache_stampede](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cache_stampede)). Surprisingly thorough; the canonical reference for the XFetch formula `time() − delta·beta·ln(rand(0,1)) ≥ expiry`.

**OneUptime — "How to Build Cache Stampede Prevention"** ([oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-01-30-cache-stampede-prevention/view](https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-01-30-cache-stampede-prevention/view)) and the sister post on Redis stampede ([2026-01-21-redis-cache-stampede](https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-01-21-redis-cache-stampede/view)). Side-by-side comparison of distributed locks, probabilistic early recomputation, and singleflight/request coalescing with Python pseudocode that translates trivially to Kotlin coroutines.

**Distributed Computing Musings — "Thundering Herd Problem: Preventing the Stampede"** ([distributed-computing-musings.com/2025/08/thundering-herd-problem-preventing-the-stampede/](https://distributed-computing-musings.com/2025/08/thundering-herd-problem-preventing-the-stampede/)). Reproduces the problem with Spring Boot + Postgres + Redis + a Go load generator, then shows the lock-based fix in real Spring code. Closer to your stack than most write-ups on this topic.

### B2. Distributed locking for cache rebuild

**Martin Kleppmann — "How to do distributed locking"** ([martin.kleppmann.com/2016/02/08/how-to-do-distributed-locking.html](https://martin.kleppmann.com/2016/02/08/how-to-do-distributed-locking.html)). Read this *before* you reach for Redlock. Argues that for correctness you need fencing tokens; Redlock's clock-drift assumptions make it unsafe for "must not happen twice" workloads. For cache-rebuild deduplication (an efficiency optimization) Redlock is fine — and that distinction is the actionable takeaway.

**Antirez — Redlock spec and rebuttal** ([redis.io/docs/latest/develop/clients/patterns/distributed-locks/](https://redis.io/docs/latest/develop/clients/patterns/distributed-locks/)). Updated to acknowledge Kleppmann's critique; the page now explicitly recommends fencing tokens for correctness-critical paths.

🇰🇷 **Kurly — "풀필먼트 입고 서비스팀에서 분산락을 사용하는 방법 - Spring Redisson"** ([helloworld.kurly.com/blog/distributed-redisson-lock/](https://helloworld.kurly.com/blog/distributed-redisson-lock/)). Direct comparison of Lettuce-based spinlock vs Redisson Pub/Sub-based lock for the cache-rebuild use case in a Spring stack. They picked Redisson because the Pub/Sub mechanism avoids the "every contender hammers Redis" failure mode of naive `SETNX` retry.

### B3. Multi-tier (L1 Caffeine + L2 Redis)

🇰🇷 **Olive Young — "고성능 캐시 아키텍처 설계 - 로컬 캐시와 Redis로 대규모 증정 행사 관리 최적화"** ([oliveyoung.tech/2024-12-10/present-promotion-multi-layer-cache/](https://oliveyoung.tech/2024-12-10/present-promotion-multi-layer-cache/)). Production case study with hard numbers: TPS +478%, ElastiCache Network Bytes Out −99.1% after adding a Caffeine L1 in front of the existing Redis L2. They explain why Redis network egress, not Redis CPU, was their actual bottleneck — a non-obvious lesson for anyone provisioning ElastiCache. Excellent walkthrough of the data-versioning scheme they used (`행사_지급_조건_v3` style versioned keys) to make atomic switchover safe.

🇰🇷 **Kakao Pay — "분산 시스템에서 로컬 캐시 활용하기"** ([tech.kakaopay.com/post/local-caching-in-distributed-systems/](https://tech.kakaopay.com/post/local-caching-in-distributed-systems/)). The reference Korean-language text on the L1+L2 with Redis Pub/Sub invalidation pattern. Walks through their `CacheManager` abstraction class, the circular-DI pitfall they hit when wiring `MessageListener` against `CacheService` and how `ApplicationEventPublisher` resolved it, and the eventual-consistency tradeoff they accepted ("each server may briefly disagree, eventually converges").

**DoorDash — "How DoorDash Standardized and Improved Microservices Caching"** ([careersatdoordash.com/blog/how-doordash-standardized-and-improved-microservices-caching/](https://careersatdoordash.com/blog/how-doordash-standardized-and-improved-microservices-caching/)). The three-tier architecture (request-local HashMap → Caffeine → Redis Lettuce) plus runtime control + shadow mode comparison. Kotlin-based, JVM stack — basically your tech stack. The shadow-mode pattern (a configurable percentage of cached responses are also fetched from source-of-truth and metric-compared) is the single best idea I've seen for safely introducing a cache into an existing system.

**Stack Overflow — "How We Do App Caching - 2019 Edition"** by Nick Craver ([nickcraver.com/blog/2019/08/06/stack-overflow-how-we-do-app-caching/](https://nickcraver.com/blog/2019/08/06/stack-overflow-how-we-do-app-caching/), also at [stackoverflow.blog](https://stackoverflow.blog/2019/08/06/how-stack-overflow-caches-apps-for-a-multi-tenant-architecture/)). Multi-tenant caching done right: per-site keyspace prefixing, two tiers ("Local Cache" in-process / "Shared Cache" Redis), protobuf serialization to dodge JSON deserialization cost on hits, and the operational discipline ("only cache if you need to"). Pair with Marc Gravell's [`StackExchange.Redis`](https://github.com/StackExchange/StackExchange.Redis) library design discussion in the same series.

**Baeldung — "Implement Two-Level Cache With Spring"** ([baeldung.com/spring-two-level-cache](https://www.baeldung.com/spring-two-level-cache)). One of the few Baeldung articles that actually adds value beyond what's in the Spring docs: it shows that Spring's `@Cacheable` does *not* re-populate L1 from L2 by default and walks through writing a `CacheInterceptor` to fix it.

**SuppieRK / `spring-boot-multilevel-cache-starter`** ([github.com/SuppieRK/spring-boot-multilevel-cache-starter](https://github.com/SuppieRK/spring-boot-multilevel-cache-starter)). Production-grade starter implementing the L1 Caffeine + L2 Redis pattern with Resilience4j circuit breaker around Redis (so Redis-down degrades gracefully to L1-only) and randomized local TTL with jitter to avoid synchronized expiry. Read the README — it's a concise design rationale doc in its own right.

**Gaetano Piazzolla — "spring-boot-multi-layer-cache"** ([github.com/GaetanoPiazzolla/spring-boot-multi-layer-cache](https://github.com/GaetanoPiazzolla/spring-boot-multi-layer-cache); long-form post at [gaetanopiazzolla.github.io/java/2025/01/27/multicache.html](https://gaetanopiazzolla.github.io/java/2025/01/27/multicache.html)). A simpler reference implementation than the multilevel-cache-starter; useful as a reading exercise.

### B4. Cache invalidation — TTL, Pub/Sub, CDC

**Gunnar Morling / Debezium — "Automating Cache Invalidation With Change Data Capture"** ([debezium.io/blog/2018/12/05/automating-cache-invalidation-with-change-data-capture/](https://debezium.io/blog/2018/12/05/automating-cache-invalidation-with-change-data-capture/)). The reference architecture for using Debezium's embedded engine inside your Spring app to evict Hibernate 2LC entries when the underlying row changes (including changes made by *other* services that don't go through your JPA layer). This is the cleanest answer to the dual-write problem when you have a Hibernate 2LC and want to keep it correct without giving up its hit rate.

**Confluent — "Implement CDC & Streaming Analytics Using Kafka & Debezium"** ([confluent.io/blog/cdc-and-streaming-analytics-using-debezium-kafka/](https://www.confluent.io/blog/cdc-and-streaming-analytics-using-debezium-kafka/)). Slightly broader framing — CDC into Kafka rather than embedded — useful when multiple services subscribe to the same change events for cache invalidation.

**Percona — "Tackling the Cache Invalidation and Cache Stampede Problem in Valkey with Debezium Platform"** ([percona.com/blog/tackling-the-cache-invalidation-and-cache-stampede-problem-in-valkey-with-debezium-platform/](https://www.percona.com/blog/tackling-the-cache-invalidation-and-cache-stampede-problem-in-valkey-with-debezium-platform/)). End-to-end MySQL → Debezium → Valkey demo. Valkey is the Redis fork; the patterns translate 1:1.

**Redis Inc. — "What's new in two: April 2025 (Redis Data Integration / RDI in private preview)"** ([redis.io/blog/whats-new-in-two-april-2025/](https://redis.io/blog/whats-new-in-two-april-2025/)). Note that this is a *vendor product announcement of a private preview* — RDI is described as "now in private preview on Redis Cloud Pro" and the page reads partly as marketing. Treat the latency claims ("milliseconds") as Redis Inc.'s figures, not independently verified. That said, the underlying idea — managed CDC-to-Redis replication so your cache and source-of-truth stay aligned without you operating the pipeline — is exactly the productized version of the Debezium pattern.

**Spring Engineering — "Case Study: Change Data Capture with CDC Debezium source and Analytics sink"** ([spring.io/blog/2020/12/14/case-study-change-data-capture-cdc-analysis-with-cdc-debezium-source-and-analytics-sink-in-real-time/](https://spring.io/blog/2020/12/14/case-study-change-data-capture-cdc-analysis-with-cdc-debezium-source-and-analytics-sink-in-real-time/)). Spring Cloud Data Flow's CDC source built on Debezium's embedded engine — useful if you're already using Spring Cloud Stream.

**Shopify — "IdentityCache" + "Cache invalidations can be missed on rollout of schema changes"** ([github.com/Shopify/identity_cache](https://github.com/Shopify/identity_cache); engineering post [shopify.engineering/identitycache-improving-performance-one-cached-model-at-a-time](https://shopify.engineering/identitycache-improving-performance-one-cached-model-at-a-time); design issue [github.com/Shopify/identity_cache/issues/535](https://github.com/Shopify/identity_cache/issues/535)). Rails, not Spring, but the *thesis* applies directly: their IdentityCache deliberately accepts that "invalidations will sometimes be lost" and is opt-in per-callsite (`#fetch` instead of `#find`) precisely so you can use it for performance-critical reads but never for money-moving paths. Shopify explicitly state they never use IdentityCache on payment flows. This is the production-grade version of the "when NOT to cache" content you asked for.

**Shopify — "Making Shopify's Flagship App 20% Faster in 6 Weeks Using a Novel Caching Solution"** ([shopify.engineering/shop-app-custom-caching-solution](https://shopify.engineering/shop-app-custom-caching-solution)). Concrete numbers: -15% database load, -20% overall app latency, double-digit CPU reduction. Custom write-through cache for a feed where read-through-with-invalidation would have invalidated too often to be useful.

### B5. Hibernate 2LC strategies

**Vlad Mihalcea — full second-level-cache series** ([vladmihalcea.com/tag/second-level-cache/](https://vladmihalcea.com/tag/second-level-cache/)). The complete tour: how 2LC stores entries (dehydrated, not as objects), each `CacheConcurrencyStrategy` (`READ_ONLY` / `NONSTRICT_READ_WRITE` / `READ_WRITE` / `TRANSACTIONAL`) explained with the failure modes, query cache N+1 issues, caching null results, collection caching. The single deep-dive read on Hibernate caching that exists.

**Vlad Mihalcea — "Things to consider before jumping to enterprise caching"** ([vladmihalcea.com](https://vladmihalcea.com/) — search the title). The "when NOT to use 2LC" companion piece: discusses why an explicit `@Cacheable`-on-DTO often beats Hibernate 2LC because you cache the shape of data the API actually returns rather than entity dehydration that you'll have to rehydrate anyway.

### B6. `@Cacheable` pitfalls (proxy / serialization / records)

🇰🇷 **Woowahan — "Spring Cache(@Cacheable) + Spring Data Redis 사용 시 record 직렬화 오류 원인과 해결"** ([techblog.woowahan.com/22767/](https://techblog.woowahan.com/22767/)). Sharp piece on the `GenericJackson2JsonRedisSerializer` × Java `record` interaction: deserialization fails because Jackson's polymorphic type-info handling doesn't emit class info for records the way it does for classes. They walk through the root cause (concerns-separation gap between Jackson and Spring Data Redis), the workarounds, and the implication that you should version your `cacheName`s when changing serialization. Highly practical for anyone running Spring Boot 3.x with Kotlin data classes / Java records.

🇰🇷 **Woowahan — "Redis New Connection 증가 이슈 돌아보기"** ([techblog.woowahan.com/23121/](https://techblog.woowahan.com/23121/)). ElastiCache's default 100-second idle-connection timeout collides with Lettuce's connection pool LIFO ordering, producing a constant trickle of new connections at low traffic. They fixed it by switching the pool to FIFO via `GenericObjectPoolConfig`. Operational gold for anyone running Spring Data Redis on AWS.

**JetBrains — `SpringCacheableMethodCallsInspection`** ([jetbrains.com/help/inspectopedia/SpringCacheableMethodCallsInspection.html](https://www.jetbrains.com/help/inspectopedia/SpringCacheableMethodCallsInspection.html)) and Baeldung — "Invoke Spring @Cacheable from Another Method of Same Bean" ([baeldung.com/spring-invoke-cacheable-other-method-same-bean](https://www.baeldung.com/spring-invoke-cacheable-other-method-same-bean)). The proxy/self-invocation problem and three remediations: self-injection, AspectJ load-time/compile-time weaving, or refactoring to a separate bean. The DZone article *Spring Boot Annotations / self-invocation problem* ([dzone.com/articles/spring-beans-self-invocation-problem](https://dzone.com/articles/spring-beans-self-invocation-problem)) is the most thorough write-up.

### B7. Reactive caching (WebFlux, Mono, coroutines)

**Baeldung — "Spring Webflux and @Cacheable Annotation"** ([baeldung.com/spring-webflux-cacheable](https://www.baeldung.com/spring-webflux-cacheable)). Explains the core trap: `@Cacheable` caches the `Mono` *wrapper*, not the resolved value, so you appear to cache but every cache hit re-subscribes to the database. The workaround is `Mono.cache()` inside the cached method.

**`rezaarshad/Spring-Webflux-Cache`** ([github.com/rezaarshad/Spring-Webflux-Cache](https://github.com/rezaarshad/Spring-Webflux-Cache)) and **`Bryksin/redis-reactive-cache`** ([github.com/Bryksin/redis-reactive-cache](https://github.com/Bryksin/redis-reactive-cache)). Reference implementations of reactive cache annotations on top of Spring Data Redis Reactive. Useful as reading exercises; both note the underlying constraint that JSR-107 and Spring's `Cache` interface are blocking.

### B8. Idempotency keys vs caching (Stripe pattern)

**Brandur Leach — "Implementing Stripe-like Idempotency Keys in Postgres"** ([brandur.org/idempotency-keys](https://brandur.org/idempotency-keys)). The canonical reference. Important because engineers frequently confuse "I'll cache the response in Redis with the idempotency key" with proper idempotency: real idempotency requires durable, transactional bookkeeping (Postgres or equivalent) plus distinguishing transient vs intrinsic errors so the layer knows which to cache. See also [`brandur.org/fragments/idempotency-keys-crunchy`](https://brandur.org/fragments/idempotency-keys-crunchy) (his updated, leaner reimplementation) and [`brandur.org/fragments/is-transient`](https://brandur.org/fragments/is-transient) on the `is_transient` error flag.

**Stripe Engineering — "Designing robust and predictable APIs with idempotency"** ([stripe.com/blog/idempotency](https://stripe.com/blog/idempotency)). The shorter, more accessible companion. Confirms keys should be short-lived (Stripe expires after 24h, Brandur recommends 1h in his reimplementation), should hash request payload to detect misuse, and that the response cache is bounded in scope.

### B9. Cache observability (Micrometer, hit ratios)

**Spring Data Redis docs — Observability** ([docs.spring.io/spring-data/redis/reference/observability.html](https://docs.spring.io/spring-data/redis/reference/observability.html)). Shows how to wire Lettuce's `MicrometerTracing` into `ClientResources` so every Redis command emits a meter and span. Combined with Spring Boot 3's Micrometer/OpenTelemetry integration this gives you per-command latency histograms with negligible config.

**Salesforce Engineering — "Lessons Learned using Spring Data Redis"** ([engineering.salesforce.com/lessons-learned-using-spring-data-redis-f3121f89bff9/](https://engineering.salesforce.com/lessons-learned-using-spring-data-redis-f3121f89bff9/)). Production lesson: Spring Data Redis `@RedisHash` repositories create a SET-based index for each hash that does *not* expire when entries TTL out, causing memory leaks. Fix is to enable keyspace-event notifications on `@EnableRedisRepositories` so the framework can listen for expirations and clean the index. Important if you use Spring Data Redis repositories rather than raw `RedisTemplate`.

🇰🇷 **Woowahan — "스토리지 최적의 스펙 관리 시스템 만들기"** ([techblog.woowahan.com/13539/](https://techblog.woowahan.com/13539/)). Operational systems-engineering view: how Woowahan automate detection of overspec'd ElastiCache instances using CloudWatch `GetTypeCmds` / `SetTypeCmds` / network-allowance-exceeded metrics. Saved ~50% of annual storage cost. Adjacent to caching-correctness but extremely valuable for anyone running large fleets.

### B10. Production case studies with numbers

🇰🇷 **Toss — "캐시를 적용하기 까지의 험난한 길 (TPS 1만 안정적으로 서비스하기)"** ([toss.tech/article/34481](https://toss.tech/article/34481)). TPS-uplift story for the Toss Bank terms-of-service server: average 10K TPS / peak 20K TPS sustained after introducing a Redis look-aside cache. They explicitly chose Redis over a read-replica database because they need *strong consistency* — replication delay would have produced incorrect "user did not consent" responses, which is unacceptable for a personal-info-sharing flow. Walk-through of cache-eviction timing using `AFTER_COMMIT` Spring application events plus a Kafka outbox pattern.

🇰🇷 **SK Devocean — "Spring Boot 성능 개선 사례 공유 (1) - Redis 및 Local 캐싱 활용"** ([devocean.sk.com/blog/techBoardDetail.do?ID=167203](https://devocean.sk.com/blog/techBoardDetail.do?ID=167203)). 2× throughput improvement at 10K concurrent requests after moving from per-parameter Redis caching to "load full dataset into Redis, then mirror into local memory, serve from local memory." Detailed treatment of the `ApplicationEventPublisher` workaround for the circular-DI problem when wiring Redis Pub/Sub `MessageListener` against `CacheService`.

🇰🇷 **SK Devocean — "Redis와 클라이언트 캐시 간 데이터 동기화 기술 - Redis Client Caching"** ([devocean.sk.com/blog/techBoardDetail.do?ID=167301](https://devocean.sk.com/blog/techBoardDetail.do?ID=167301)). Walks through Redis 6+ *server-assisted client-side caching* (the `CLIENT TRACKING` command) — a less-known but powerful alternative to Pub/Sub-based invalidation that pushes invalidation messages directly to clients on key changes.

🇰🇷 **Kakao — "if(kakao)2020 코멘터리 01: 카카오톡 캐싱 시스템의 진화"** ([tech.kakao.com/posts/406](https://tech.kakao.com/posts/406)) and **"쿠버네티스에 레디스 캐시 클러스터 구축기"** ([tech.kakao.com/2022/02/09/k8s-redis/](https://tech.kakao.com/2022/02/09/k8s-redis/)). The KakaoTalk caching evolution series — moving from on-premise Redis clusters to Kubernetes-orchestrated Redis cache farms, including the sports-event traffic-spike story that motivated the migration.

🇰🇷 **Kakao — "분산 웹 캐시 (Wcache)의 개선과정"** parts 1 and 2 ([tech.kakao.com/2017/10/23/wcache-2/](https://tech.kakao.com/2017/10/23/wcache-2/)). Kakao's home-grown HTTP-level distributed cache. Long, low-level, but the discussion of read/write lock granularity, vary-header handling, and JBF (Just a Big File) group rotation to avoid synchronized cold-cache events is genuinely instructive even if you'll never write a Wcache yourself.

🇰🇷 **Kurly — "DevOps 엔지니어의 Redis Test 분투기 - Part 1"** ([helloworld.kurly.com/blog/redis-fight-part-1/](https://helloworld.kurly.com/blog/redis-fight-part-1/)). Cache-sizing methodology: how Kurly figured out the right ElastiCache instance size by load-testing the look-aside pattern at 200 concurrent users and watching CPU on the read endpoint during cache miss + set storms. Honest engineering blog with measurements rather than abstract advice.

🇰🇷 **Woowahan — "배차 정확도를 높이는 실거리 시스템 구축하기: OSRM, Kafka, 그리고 Redis"** ([techblog.woowahan.com/22396/](https://techblog.woowahan.com/22396/)). Why they chose Redis Hash (`HMSET`/`HMGET` per region ID + coordinate pair) over a serialized String: the per-field TTL semantics, network-bandwidth reduction, and how Redis Cluster sharding by region ID balanced write load. Includes the mistake of setting `EXPIRE` on every `HMSET` (which constantly resets TTL) and the fix.

**DoorDash — "How We Applied Client-Side Caching to Improve Feature Store Performance by 70%"** ([doordash.engineering/2022/05/03/how-we-applied-client-side-caching/](https://doordash.engineering/2022/05/03/how-we-applied-client-side-caching/)). Validates a caching idea via *traffic capture and offline simulation* before any production rollout. Reports >70% hit rate matching simulation. The methodology — capture-and-replay against a candidate cache to predict hit rate — is reusable wherever you're considering adding a cache.

**Netflix Tech Blog — EVCache series** ([netflixtechblog.com/announcing-evcache-distributed-in-memory-datastore-for-cloud-c26a698c27f7](https://netflixtechblog.com/announcing-evcache-distributed-in-memory-datastore-for-cloud-c26a698c27f7), [netflixtechblog.com/caching-for-a-global-netflix-7bcc457012f1](https://netflixtechblog.com/caching-for-a-global-netflix-7bcc457012f1), and the cache-warming follow-ups [netflixtechblog.medium.com/cache-warming-leveraging-ebs-for-moving-petabytes-of-data-adcf7a4a78c3](https://netflixtechblog.medium.com/cache-warming-leveraging-ebs-for-moving-petabytes-of-data-adcf7a4a78c3)). 30M req/s, ~2T req/day, multi-region replication, EBS-multi-attach for petabyte-scale cache warming, 99.9%+ hit rate on hot services. You will not build EVCache, but reading it calibrates your sense of what "caching at scale" actually means.

### B11. Java/Spring tutorials worth reading at the architectural level

**Spring Boot reference — Caching** ([docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/io/caching.html](https://docs.spring.io/spring-boot/reference/io/caching.html)) and **Spring Data Redis — Redis Cache** ([docs.spring.io/spring-data/redis/reference/redis/redis-cache.html](https://docs.spring.io/spring-data/redis/reference/redis/redis-cache.html)). The reference docs have aged into the most useful sources because they capture the post-2020 changes Plöd doesn't cover: per-cache TTL via `RedisCacheManagerBuilderCustomizer`, `TtlFunction` for dynamic per-entry TTLs, lock-free vs locking `RedisCacheWriter` and the throughput tradeoff, and `SCAN`-based batch strategy for `clear()` to avoid `KEYS` performance disasters at large keyspaces.

**Reflectoring — "Implementing a Cache with Spring Boot"** ([reflectoring.io/spring-boot-cache/](https://reflectoring.io/spring-boot-cache/)) by Tom Hombergs. The architecturally-focused tutorial, with Hazelcast as the example provider — closer in spirit to Plöd's enterprise-grid framing.

---

## C. Suggested Curriculum: A Multi-Week Path

This is structured as four "weeks" but treat them as logical units; pace as you like.

**Week 1 — What's changed since Plöd's 2016 talk.** Start with Ben Manes's *Design of a Modern Cache* on High Scalability, then the Caffeine wiki Design and Efficiency pages; this rewires the "which local cache" intuition (Caffeine + W-TinyLFU is the new default, not EhCache). Read the Spring Boot reference's Caching chapter and the Spring Data Redis Redis-Cache chapter to see what Spring Boot 3 / Spring Framework 6 added — per-cache TTL builders, `TtlFunction`, lock-free writer, SCAN-based clear, observability via Lettuce + Micrometer. Finish with Joris Kuipers's *It's all in the mix* talk from Spring I/O 2024 to see what production-readiness looks like in 2024 idiom. By end of Week 1 you have an updated mental model of the Spring Cache abstraction landscape.

**Week 2 — The five problems Plöd punted on.** Read the Wikipedia *Cache stampede* article for the formal model, then Toss's `cache-traffic-tip` post and LINE's Lua-PER post for two production implementations. Read Kleppmann's *How to do distributed locking* and the antirez rebuttal — internalize the fencing-token argument before reaching for Redlock. Read Brandur Leach's *Implementing Stripe-like Idempotency Keys in Postgres* so you can confidently distinguish "I want to deduplicate writes" (idempotency, durable Postgres bookkeeping) from "I want to deduplicate reads" (caching). Read DoorDash's *How DoorDash Standardized and Improved Microservices Caching* for the multi-tier reference architecture and the shadow-mode introduction technique. By end of Week 2 you know what questions to ask before adding any cache.

**Week 3 — Hibernate 2LC and the "cache vs database" interface.** Read Vlad Mihalcea's full second-level-cache series in order, with particular attention to the four `CacheConcurrencyStrategy` posts and the *Things to consider before jumping to enterprise caching* piece. Watch the *High-Performance Hibernate* talk on YouTube. Read Debezium's *Automating Cache Invalidation With Change Data Capture* for the CDC-based 2LC eviction pattern. Read Toss's 약관 server piece for a non-Hibernate alternative (use Redis directly with `AFTER_COMMIT` Spring events for eviction). Read Shopify's IdentityCache README + the *Cache invalidations can be missed* design issue to understand why IdentityCache is opt-in per call site and explicitly forbidden on payment paths. By end of Week 3 you can articulate when 2LC, when Spring `@Cacheable`, when CDC-driven invalidation, and when "do not cache."

**Week 4 — Operations, observability, and idiom for your stack.** Read the Korean-language production case studies in this order: Olive Young's multi-layer post (numbers: TPS +478%, network egress −99.1%), Kakao Pay's distributed local cache piece (architecture and Pub/Sub), SK Devocean's two-part Spring Boot performance posts, and Woowahan's record-serialization and Redis-new-connection posts. These are densely applied and use exactly your stack (Spring Boot, Lettuce, ElastiCache). Then read the Spring Data Redis observability docs and the Salesforce *Lessons Learned* post to set up Micrometer-based hit-rate tracking and avoid the `@RedisHash` SET memory leak. Finish with the Stack Overflow series by Nick Craver and the Netflix EVCache series for scale calibration. By end of Week 4 you have a defensible production cache design and the operational vocabulary to explain it to your team.

**Optional Week 5 — Reactive and Kotlin-specific.** If you're using WebFlux, work through the Baeldung `@Cacheable` + Mono article and the two reactive-cache reference repos to internalize the wrapper-vs-value caching trap. Coroutine-specific material is sparse; the closest analog is the singleflight pattern documented in OneUptime's stampede post, which translates directly to a `Mutex`-guarded `ConcurrentHashMap<K, Deferred<V>>` in Kotlin.

---

## Caveats and Gaps

A few honest disclosures based on this research process. First, **Michael Plöd's recorded caching talks all derive from a single body of work** (SpringOne 2GX 2014 → Spring I/O 2016 → Spring Meetup Munich 2017). I could find no evidence of a refreshed follow-on talk; from 2018 his speaking pivoted to DDD. Second, **several speakers on your shortlist do not appear to have recorded conference talks specifically on Spring caching**: Marc Gravell, Stéphane Nicoll, Juergen Hoeller, Oliver Drotbohm/Gierke, Josh Long, and Dan Vega all have related material in blog or short-video form, but no full conference session I could verify. Ben Manes's deepest treatment of W-TinyLFU is the High Scalability essay and the Caffeine wiki, not a recorded talk. Third, **I could not confidently locate Korean-conference video recordings** (DEVIEW, if(kakao), Toss SLASH, Coupang Tech Conference) specifically on Spring caching — the Korean tech ecosystem produces this material more in blog form than as conference videos, and the slides+blog combination at the Toss/Kakao Pay/Olive Young/Kurly/Woowahan/SK Devocean blogs is generally where this content lives. Fourth, **Redis Inc.'s RDI announcement** is presented in their own marketing voice on their own blog, with the latency claim ("milliseconds") not independently verified — I've flagged this in the body. Fifth, **the Redlock / Kleppmann debate is unresolved** in the sense that both sides have legitimate points; for cache-rebuild deduplication (efficiency) Redlock is fine, but treat it as load-shedding, not as a correctness primitive. Finally, this list reflects a research budget of approximately 20 searches; deeper investigation of Devoxx archives, GeeCON, JFokus, and JavaLand might surface additional talks, but the speakers most strongly associated with Spring caching (Plöd, Kuipers, Mihalcea, Paluch, Manes) are well-covered above.

The list is intentionally biased toward applied and production content, which means a few important academic references are absent — most notably the original *TinyLFU* (Einziger, Friedman, Manes) and *xfetch* (Vattani, Chierichetti, Lowenstein) papers, which you can access through the citations in the High Scalability post and the Wikipedia Cache stampede page if you want to go deeper into the algorithmic foundations.
